author: @CyberDnl
text: Thank you, I always enjoy your videos. The period is only given by the length of the pendulum and the gravitational force. The mass has no effect on it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Great point - but I suspect this is only an approximation - after all the regulate the pendulum of the clock in the Elizabeth Tower in London (Big Ben) by putting pennies on it - the addition of weight changes the beat of the pendulum by 2/5 of a second every day - of course that pendulum is massive - around 4.4M long and 310kg - the bob is 204kg - having said adding weight does make a difference to the beat

replies.author: @CyberDnl
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering It should be irrelevant from the maths/physics behind it. Maybe the increased friction from the pennies in the airflow tuned it in ;-). 
BTW: I also own a 3D Printer and enjoy it, but I also miss the old videos, when you did the builds with alternative tools. It was nice to see, how something startet to get it's shape. With 3D printing, the results are on the table from the first second of the video. But I still learn a lot from you. Keep up your positive nature.

author: @Tony-r8k
text: In the past I've wondered if it would be possible to design an escapement mechanism with the proper gear ratios, tooth count, timing, etc. and probably even with slip gears where the main wheel is pulled with let's say a 2 lb weight while it's pulling up a 1.25 lb weight and when the 1.25 lb weight reaches the top it disconnects and then a 2nd 1.25 lb weight goes through the same motion and when they both are raised to the top they engage each other to become a 2.5 lb weight in order to raise the now lowered 2 lb weight and start all over again when I put it on paper it looked plausible I think Mr. Smith could be the guy to do it if it's possible.. just some food for thought Mr. Smith

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate that doesn't look plausible to me - How does the second weight get pulled up? when the cycle has finished you would have 2 pounds at the top and 2.5 pounds at the bottom - it wouldn't repeat

replies.author: @Tony-r8k
replies.text: Like in the beginning the 2 (1.25 lb weights) disengage each other

replies.author: @Tony-r8k
replies.text: It would be about a quarter cycle of the big wheel to pull up the 1.25 lb weight

author: @salimufari
text: Material choices are important for improving these designs once you learn the mechanics. The biggest problem with rubber as a material is as soon as you stretch it the power drops. As it is stretched you lose elasticity in heat & ruptured fibers. Constant force / clockwork springs are much better for energy storage.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh for sure but this is really about the mechanics - using a rubber band is just for fun the same principles apply - and a rubber band is certainly better than a PLA spring which is what lots of these print projects use

author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
text:
Hello sir Robert!
I've been trying to look onto a very similar subject.
But I have no idea on where to start the research, or how to properly articulate my dillema.
I've been looking forever into how to make a sort of a valve to release pneumatic energy in a controlled manner.
And at a particular flow rate and power.
But for the life of me, I'm very concerned about the energy losses that would occur.
And I think the shearing of the fluid, while being released.. through the valve.. would cause major energy losses.
And I just can't find a solution to counteract these.
Pneumatic/compressed air can be a very widely available, cheap and practical form of energy storage solutions.
Especially acting great as a mechanical capacitor for fast reactions.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't understand mate - it is hard to visualize a problem in isolation - what is it you want to do with the compressed air?

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
@ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that helps - if a problem can't be solved from one side tackle it from the other. I suppose you could attach a flywheel and when the pressure drops in one tank switch to another - the flywheel will even out rotation while you make the switch

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering that's a pretty good idea.
To buffer the energy output.
Also, sir.
I have a few concepts at hand that may require some effort.. making.
But I think they're almost breakthrough worthy.
And maybe worth taking a look at.
I know you mention a lack of interest at conceptual projects.
But I really don't have the resources to make them at present.
Could you be open to evaluate my idea. It'd sure take me a couple of days to get upto speed on fluid simulations and whatnot, and build the model.
But when I'm done, would you take a look?
I will be very grateful it for if you do.
Those designs involve few things.
1. One being a sea wave energy harvester.
2. Another is a motor.
3. And an engine.
I'm most excited for the motor.
But I just love the engine, such a simple design.. which is something I think inspired by you and how you simplify your iterations such that they can be made with minimal tools.
Although only the principle of how it works is simple (the engine), making it would require some machinery.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am glad it helped - as for looking at your other ideas - i would be happy to if you make them and do a video - otherwise - no, I am afraid not.

author: @frankdutton9852
text: The weight of the Bob of the pendulum has no bearing on the period of the motion. It is only the effective length of the pendulum that determins the periodicity

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you are quite right i put this badly - Changing the mass does affect the amount of gravitational potential energy at the end of each swing, and the amount of kinetic energy at the midpoint of the swing. Increased mass of the bob would increase inertia and allow the pendulum to swing longer before friction brings it to rest - but I suspect this is only an approximation - after all the regulate the pendulum of the clock in the Elizabeth Tower in London (Big Ben) by putting pennies on it - the addition of weight changes the beat of the pendulum by 2/5 of a second every day - of course that pendulum is massive - around 4.4M long and 310kg - the bob is 204kg - having said adding weight does make a difference to the beat

author: @kandsgibson
text:
Good day Robert.
I have an intriguing question.  Off topic of today's video, though.  Hope you don't mind.
Firstly, a bit of background.
Our home has a “high-efficiency” forced-air furnace with a drum type fan. (Natural gas for heat!)
The fan runs constantly, at a lower level to circulate air, then it ramps up to full speed when the thermostat determines we need more heat. 
The fan is ALWAYS running.
This is the most common type of furnace in our area.
So, here’s my question.
Could a generator be added to one (or both) sides of the fan to generate power?
(ie – glue magnets to the edge of the fan, then somehow add a copper winding, etc.)
I guess my point is that the fan is always running so why not try to harvest some energy out of it?
What do you think??
Cheers mate
Kendall
A big fan of yours.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the load you put on the fan by trying to scavenge energy will just increase the energy bill mate as there is no external input - why is it always on? Maybe just switch it off? i don't know - I don't know why they would do that - is there a reason?
